compiled_state.title: Review workflow escalates to user instead of escalating to higher-tier reviewer — should exhaust all reviewer tiers before user escalation
compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart
compiled_state.description:
## What happened

In a host project, when a code-reviewer-light (haiku) review failed or produced issues, the agent escalated directly to the user asking for permission to bypass, rather than escalating to a higher-tier reviewer (standard/sonnet, then deep/opus).

## Expected behavior

The review resolution loop should exhaust all reviewer tiers before escalating to the user:
1. If light-tier (haiku) review fails or produces issues → escalate to standard-tier (sonnet)
2. If standard-tier review fails → escalate to deep-tier (3 sonnet + opus)
3. Only after exhausting all tiers → escalate to user

Any review that produces issues inherently deserves more scrutiny, not less. A single light-tier failure should cause ALL subsequent reviews in the session to use at least standard tier.

## Evidence from host project

After the review agent had issues, it asked the user: 'Can I commit this merge with the pre-commit hooks bypassed?' rather than trying a higher-tier reviewer. The agent rationalized that the merge 'just resolves conflicts' and the prior batches were 'individually reviewed.'

## Fix needed

1. Review resolution loop must escalate reviewer tier before escalating to user
2. After any review failure, set a session-level floor: minimum standard tier for all subsequent reviews
3. The 'escalate to user' path should only be reachable after the deep tier has been attempted

comments.body: INTERACTIVITY_DEFERRED: safeguard_approval | LLM-behavioral: review escalation skips tiers. Fix requires editing docs/workflows/REVIEW-WORKFLOW.md (safeguard). Re-run in interactive session to approve.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart

comments.body: Bot-psychologist RESULT: FAIL/ESCALATE dispatch table says escalate to user with no pointer to tier-upgrade gate. Fix: Add tier-upgrade check to dispatch entries.
comments.author: Joe Oakhart
